1:
The witnesses set the scene with one voice and then divide over what to make of it. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the moment. Adam Clarke dates the rest to the interval after David had cleared the Philistines out of the strong places of Israel but before he carried his arms abroad against Moab, Syria, and Edom (see 2 Samuel 8:1-14). Ellicott cautions that the narrative is placed here by subject, not strict chronology: the historian, having told of the ark's removal, records David's further purposes in the same direction, and the episode belongs to a season of quiet prosperity before the troubles of his latter years. Keil and Delitzsch survey the whole chapter at a stroke — David, at rest, resolves to build a house for the Lord, is sanctioned by Nathan, then learns that God will first build a house for him.

The reformed Gill and the wesleyan Wesley agree on the plain sense: David is now settled in the palace Hiram's servants had built, with no occasion to take the field, and turns his thoughts from his own ease to the unsettled state of the ark.

It is the puritan Matthew Henry who draws out the heart of the verse. Though a man of war, David was for peace (Ps. 120:7) and in his element sitting in his house meditating in the law of God. His thought of building a temple is a grateful return: when God has done much for us, it should set us contriving what we may do for him. Henry contrasts David's musing with Nebuchadnezzar pacing his palace and thinking only of his own majesty (Dan. 4:30) — the one proud, the other full of contrivance how to glorify God, and God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble. Gracious souls, Henry adds, never think they can do enough for God, and cannot enjoy their own accommodations while the church lies under a cloud: David can take little pleasure in a house of cedar unless the ark have one, unlike those who stretched themselves on beds of ivory and were not grieved for the affliction of Joseph (Amos 6:4-6). The witnesses do not contend here; together they make David's rest the occasion not of luxury but of holy discontent, a king uneasy that his God is worse housed than himself.
2:
David's words to Nathan — I dwell in a house of cedar, but the ark dwells within curtains — draw the witnesses to the man, the incongruity, and the vocabulary. The grammatical-historical commentators do the close work. Ellicott identifies Nathan as already a confidential counsellor of the king, prominent later in this reign and at the opening of Solomon's (compare his part in 1 Kings 1:11-38), and one who, with Gad the seer, wrote parts of this history (1 Chronicles 29:29). He notes that 'within curtains' is the very word used for the covering of the tabernacle in Exodus 26, and sets David's holy unease against the opposite temper of the returned exiles who let God's house lie waste (Haggai 1:4). Ellicott adds a point of some care: the ark was not now within the old Mosaic tabernacle at all, but in a similar temporary structure David had newly pitched for it — so the contrast is not between two ancient tents but between the king's fresh cedar palace and a makeshift shelter for the throne of God. Clarke fills in the picture: a house of cedar is one whose beams, ceiling, and wainscot were cedar, while the ark had no residence but the tent covered with skins of beasts (Exodus 26:14).

The reformed Gill lingers on Nathan himself. This is the first mention of the prophet, a man of piety and prudence, endowed with a prophetic spirit and familiar enough with David to be told what he had been meditating in his heart. Gill records — and dismisses as unlikely — the Jewish tradition that Nathan was Jonathan the son of Shimea, David's brother's son.

The wesleyan Wesley glosses the contrast simply: the curtains are the tent or tabernacle of several hangings. Across the schools the point is one and undivided — David's heart is moved by comparing his own royal residence with the mean provision for the ark, and out of that comparison the whole covenant of this chapter will flow. The witnesses make David's discontent exemplary: a man who cannot rest in cedar while the symbol of God's presence is lodged under skins, and who takes his trouble first of all to a prophet rather than acting on his own devout impulse.
3:
On Nathan's quick endorsement — Go, do all that is in thine heart, for the Lord is with thee — the witnesses are strikingly united, and their agreement is itself the point. All four insist that the prophet here spoke as a godly man, not as a mouthpiece of God. The reformed Gill puts it plainly: Nathan perceived the good design and, knowing a house would be built at some time by someone, encouraged it; but because the time and the builder had not been pointed out in the word of God, he and David erred in not consulting the Lord, and were tacitly reproved. Prophets, Gill observes, did not always speak under inspiration, but sometimes, as private men, said things ignorantly and by mistake.

The wesleyan voices press the same lesson. Wesley says Nathan hastily approved the pious design before consulting God, as both he and David ought to have done in a matter of such moment; and therefore the prophet meets with a rebuke that forces him to acknowledge and recant his error — for the holy prophets did not speak all things by prophetic inspiration, but some things by a human spirit. Clarke agrees exactly: Nathan gave his judgment as a pious and prudent man, not as a prophet, for the prophets were under a divine afflatus only at select times; his 'the Lord is with thee' meant no more than that David enjoyed God's blessing in all he did.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott states the principle without the note of reproof: Nathan naturally considered that it must be right for David to execute so pious a purpose, but he spoke according to his own sense of right and not by divine direction. Here, then, the schools do not divide so much as shade one another — Gill and Wesley hear a fault to be corrected, Clarke and Ellicott a limitation to be understood — yet all draw the same sober conclusion. Even a true prophet's warmest counsel is not to be mistaken for the voice of God until God has spoken; the good and the pious may still be beside the divine will, and the safest zeal waits for a warrant. That this generous mistake is corrected the very same night (v.4) only underscores the tenderness with which God guards both his prophet and his king from a well-meant error.
4:
The witnesses read the coming of the word 'that night' as an act of divine tenderness as much as of correction. The reformed Gill notes the mercy of the timing: the message came the same night following the conversation, that neither prophet nor king might continue long in their error, and especially lest David, in his great zeal and warm affection, should take some hasty and improper step. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the same swiftness and adds the crucial point of authentication: the revelation is distinctly stamped as coming from a source outside the prophet himself, since it stands in direct opposition to the very view Nathan had expressed only hours before. A man does not overturn his own confident counsel by the next morning unless God has spoken.

The puritan Matthew Henry dwells on the wisdom of the arrangement. God might have spoken to David immediately, but he chose to send the message by Nathan — first, by the same hand that had given the encouragement, lest, coming by another, the prophet be despised and the king perplexed, encouraged by one prophet and discouraged by another; and second, the same night, that Nathan might not continue in an error nor David have his head further filled with thoughts of what he must never accomplish. In this, Henry says, God supports the honour of his prophets and preserves in David a regard for them: though the king is the head, the prophets are the eyes by which he sees the visions of the Almighty and the tongue by which he hears the word of God.

Henry also catches the graciousness underneath the refusal. God took notice of David's purpose, for he knows what is in man, and was well pleased with it — as he would later testify, 'thou didst well that it was in thine heart' (1 Kings 8:18) — even while he forbade its execution. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the same night which dashed David's plan proved the reality of the revelation and the kindness of the God who corrects his servants promptly, by the gentlest possible means, and without a word of blame for the love that prompted the mistake.
5:
The question 'Shalt thou build me a house?' is heard by all the witnesses as a courteous negative, and the debate they open is not over its force but over its reason. The grammatical-historical commentators establish the grammar first. Clarke states flatly that the interrogative here has the force of 'Thou shalt not,' and Ellicott confirms it from the parallel, where the sense is made explicit — 'thou shalt not build me an house' (1 Chronicles 17:4) — as the LXX and Syriac also render it.

Ellicott then presses the tender note struck by the title. David is called 'my servant,' a name reserved for the eminent and faithful — for Moses and for Joshua — so that the whole message, and this word above all, shows the prohibition carried nothing of divine displeasure. The reformed Gill hears it the same way: the Lord speaks honourably and respectfully of David, owning him his servant in other things though he would not employ him in this, and accepting the good will even where he declines the work.

Why, then, the refusal? Ellicott is careful here. No reason is assigned in this passage; the ground appears only later, in David's own charge to Solomon and the elders — 'thou hast shed much blood upon the earth,' 'thou hast been a man of war' (1 Chronicles 22:8; 1 Chronicles 28:3). Those wars had been necessary and were never disapproved of God, yet the mere fact that David was a man of blood unfitted him for this sacred office. Ellicott further notes the two reasons commonly supplied by interpreters — that God must first build David a house before David could build his, and that the kingdom was not yet peaceful enough for a temple — but insists that neither is stated in the divine word, and that it is better to keep to what Scripture actually assigns, however fitting these other considerations may be. The witnesses thus stand together on the substance: the 'No' is real but it is not rejection; the God who declines David's cedar honours David as his servant and reserves the temple for hands unstained by war.
6:
God's own account — 'I have not dwelt in any house since I brought up Israel out of Egypt, but have walked in a tent' — is briefly glossed by the reformed Gill (a fixed, stated habitation is meant) and by the wesleyan Wesley, who distinguishes the two terms: the 'tent' may note the inner curtains and hangings, the 'tabernacle' the frame of boards and coverings over them. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, take 'in a dwelling' as explanatory of 'in a tent' — a tent that was God's dwelling — and draw the vivid inference that, as a tent is a traveller's abode, so, as long as God's house was a tent, he himself appeared as one journeying from place to place among his people.

The fullest reading comes from the puritan Matthew Henry, who turns the divine self-description into a rebuke of all mere outward pomp in worship. God, he observes, never complained of the tent; his presence was as surely with his people when the ark was in curtains as when it stood in a temple. He did not dwell but walk, and yet fainted not nor was weary. Henry lifts the image to Christ, who, like the ark, walked here on earth in a tabernacle, going about doing good and having no house of his own until he ascended on high to the mansions of his Father's house, and there sat down. The church too, like the ark, is in this world ambulatory, dwelling in a tent because her present state is both pastoral and military; her continuing city is yet to come.

From this Henry draws the searching moral: wise and good men value not the show while they have the substance, and David perhaps had more true devotion and sweeter communion with God in a house of curtains than any of his successors enjoyed in the house of cedar. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that God's willingness to walk in a tent for so many generations exposes the smallness of measuring his presence by the grandeur of its housing, and quietly prepares the way for the great reversal — that God, who needed no house, will build one for David.
7:
This verse turns on a single-letter textual question, and the grammatical-historical commentators carry it. God protests that in all his walking with Israel he never asked any of 'the tribes' why they had not built him a house of cedar — but the parallel in Chronicles reads 'judges.' Clarke explains the crux precisely: the two Hebrew words, shibtey (tribes) and shophetey (judges), differ only by one letter easily confused in a manuscript, and 'judges' is probably the true reading, since 1 Chronicles 17:6 has it and v.11 speaks of the judges. Ellicott concurs but is less quick to emend: even 'tribes' can stand for the judges sprung from them, a usage found elsewhere (Psalms 78:67-68; 1 Chronicles 28:4). Keil and Delitzsch note that the ones addressed were those 'whom I commanded to feed my people Israel' — the shepherds of the nation, whether called tribes or judges — and that God had laid no such charge on any of them.

The reformed Gill contents himself with pointing to the places where God had walked with his people, and Keil adds that the shepherds addressed were charged 'to feed my people Israel' — a pastoral trust, not a building commission. But the theological weight of the verse is best drawn out by the puritan Matthew Henry, whose exposition here is a small treatise on instituted worship: that worship only is acceptable which is appointed, and God had never ordered a temple, so why should David design what God never commanded? Let him wait for a warrant, and then act — for better a tent of God's appointing than a temple of his own inventing.

The debate among the witnesses is genuine but narrow: whether the received 'tribes' should be corrected to 'judges' (Clarke) or defended as a legitimate synecdoche (Ellicott). On the substance they are agreed. God's long silence about a house is not neglect but design; he had never solicited a cedar temple from anyone, and David's holy impulse, however good, must bow to the truth that God is to be served in his own way and at his own bidding, not by the well-meant inventions of even the most devout of kings.
8:
With 'I took thee from the sheepcote,' the promise turns from what David may not do to what God has already done, and the witnesses read it as a lesson in grace preceding merit. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark the solemn escalation of the divine name: not simply 'Jehovah' as in v.5, but 'Jehovah of hosts,' the God of the whole universe, who now discloses his gracious purpose. He took David from the pasturage behind the flock to be prince over Israel, was with him wherever he went, cut off his enemies before him, and made him a great name. Ellicott adds a small but pointed philological correction — the word rendered 'sheepcote' is better 'pasture,' the open grazing-ground — keeping the image of the shepherd-boy called straight from the fields.

The reformed Gill and the wesleyan Wesley both hear in the verse a word of reassurance. Gill sees God heaping up the tokens of his favour precisely so that David, forbidden the temple, should not think himself slighted: it was honour enough that God had raised him from a low estate to great dignity. Wesley says the same of the title 'my servant' repeated here — lest David be discouraged or judge himself neglected as one unworthy of so great an honour, God gives him the honourable name, signifying that he accepted his service and his good intentions.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the devotional point: it is good for those who have come to great preferment to be often reminded of their small beginnings, that they may remain humble and thankful, for God took David from the sheepcote and whatever the king would do for God's honour, God had first done for him. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the recital of grace is not a reproach but a comfort — God rehearses his past kindness to a servant he has just refused, so that the refusal itself is wrapped in the memory of an unearned exaltation, and David is taught that he stands wholly in the debt of a God who was beforehand with him from the pasture onward.
9:
'I was with thee whithersoever thou wentest' gathers David's whole history into one clause, and the witnesses trace it back over his life. The reformed Gill enumerates the occasions of that accompanying presence: when David went against Goliath, when he went forth against the Philistines, when he stood in Saul's court, and when he fled from Saul into place after place — in all of it God was protecting, preserving, and prospering him everywhere and in everything. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read on into the promise it introduces, noting how the perfect verbs of past mercy ('I was with thee... have cut off all thine enemies... have made thee a great name, like the name of the great men of the earth') become the ground for the future 'house' God is about to pledge.

The puritan Matthew Henry, whose exposition here reaches back to the same verse, distinguishes the two mercies. First, God had given David success and victory — 'I was with thee to protect thee when pursued, to prosper thee when pursuing,' cutting off all the enemies who stood in the way of his advancement. Second, God had crowned him not only with dominion in Israel but with reputation among the nations, making him a great name, famous for his courage, conduct, and achievements, more talked of than any of the great men of his day.

Yet Henry sets a careful proportion on this last gift. A great name, he says, is something those who have it have great reason to be thankful for and may improve to good purposes, but which those who lack it have no reason to covet — for a good name is more desirable than a great one, and a man may pass through the world very obscurely and yet very comfortably. The witnesses do not contend; they agree that God's rehearsal of David's rise is designed to fix the king's eyes on the giver rather than the gifts. The victories were God's doing, the fame was God's bestowing, and the whole ascent from the sheepfold to a name among the earth's great is a monument not to David's prowess but to the God who was with him at every step.
10:
The promise now widens from the king to the kingdom, and the witnesses divide gently over a point of Hebrew tense. The verse pledges a place for Israel where they may be planted and afflicted no more — but is that pledge future or already fulfilled? The grammatical-historical commentators press the grammar. Ellicott insists there is no change of tense in the original and would read 'have appointed... have planted,' taking the words as God's recital of what he has now accomplished under David, not a fresh prophecy. Clarke reads it the same way — 'I have appointed a place, and have planted them' — and (as his long note on v.25 argues at length) makes this past sense decisive against those who would refer the verse to some later, unfulfilled prosperity of the Jews.

The reformed Gill takes the place to be the land of Canaan, appointed of old but never wholly possessed while the Canaanites still inhabited it; now, he says, they should be expelled and the Israelites have the land to themselves. The wesleyan Wesley reads 'appoint' as 'establish them in their land,' and dwells on why Israel's welfare belongs in a promise to David: the strength and happiness of a king consists in the multitude and happiness of his people, and David, being a man of public spirit, was no less affected with Israel's felicity than with his own. He glosses the 'aforetime' of their affliction as pointing back to Egypt.

The puritan Matthew Henry sums the double blessing — a quiet place and the quiet enjoyment of it — with the children of wickedness (chiefly the Philistines, so long a plague) no more afflicting them, as in the settled days when God gave rest under the judges. The debate over the tense is real but not divisive: whether one reads 'I will appoint' or 'I have appointed,' the witnesses agree that God folds the security of his people into the covenant with their king, so that David's house and Israel's rest rise or fall together, and the good of the nation is made an article of the promise to the throne.
11:
Here the chapter pivots on a play of words the witnesses all delight in: David proposed to build God a house, and God answers that he will build David a house. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch frame the exchange as more than a happy turn of phrase — it carries the deep general truth that God must first build a man's house before the man can build God's. As long as Israel's possession of Canaan was disputed by enemies round about, even the dwelling of their God could take no form but a wanderer's tent; the kingdom of God among them first found its rest through David, once God had subdued his foes. So the temple must wait upon the throne, and the throne is God's gift.

The wesleyan voices unfold the promised 'house' as David's posterity and continuing kingdom. Clarke draws the symmetry sharply: 'Thou hast in thy heart to make me a house; I have it in my heart to make thee a house' — thy family shall be built up and prosper on the throne of Israel, and thy spiritual posterity shall remain for ever; for God is the author of all our holy purposes as well as our good works, and if we are workers together with him he will crown them as though they were our own, though he alone is their author. Wesley adds a qualification and a comfort: the promise to Israel holds 'except they should notoriously forsake God,' while the promise to David himself — a sure house — means God will increase and uphold his posterity and continue the kingdom in his family.

The puritan Matthew Henry lifts the encouragement to a maxim: whatever we do for God, or sincerely design though providence prevents our doing it, we shall in no wise lose our reward; God had promised to make David a name (v.9), and now promises to make him a house that shall bear up that name. The reformed Gill simply marks where the parenthesis about Israel closes and the promise to David resumes. The witnesses are wholly agreed: the refusal of v.5 is answered by a gift immeasurably greater, and the God who would not take a house from David's hands insists on building one for him instead.
12:
'When thy days be fulfilled... I will set up thy seed after thee' opens the dynastic heart of the promise, and the witnesses attend to its immediate reference and its longer reach. The grammatical-historical Ellicott judges that the promise certainly has immediate reference to Solomon, and weighs a question of date: many think the future tense — 'which shall proceed out of thy bowels' — shows that Solomon was not yet born, which, if so, would fix the point of David's reign to which the passage belongs. But Ellicott is cautious: the same expression might have been used after Solomon's birth, the future being merely assimilated to the futures running through the whole passage, whose point is simply that David's son shall succeed to his throne.

The reformed Gill glosses 'when thy days be fulfilled' of the natural term of David's life, which he exactly filled, living just seventy years. The wesleyan Wesley draws out the tenderness folded into the phrase: it implies that David's days should be prolonged to the usual course of nature and not cut off in the midst by any violent or untimely death — he should sleep with his fathers in peace. Wesley then lays down the interpretive rule that will govern the rest of the oracle: 'I will set up thy seed' embraces David's posterity in general, part of it belonging to Solomon, and part to the Messiah only, according to the different nature of the several passages that follow.

The debate is quiet and friendly — whether the future tense proves Solomon unborn (a real but uncertain indication of date), on which Ellicott canvasses both sides without deciding. Beneath it the witnesses agree on the shape of the thing: the promise looks first to a literal son who will succeed David and reign, and beyond him down a line whose ultimate term no mere son of David could fill. This layered reading, most explicit in Wesley, prepares the reader to hear the coming clauses now of Solomon, now of David's house at large, and now of Christ alone, as the sense of each requires.
13:
'He shall build an house for my name... I will establish the throne of his kingdom for ever' is read by every witness on two levels at once, and their agreement on the double sense is the striking thing. The reformed Gill states it in a line: the builder is Solomon, who notably did build the temple, and his antitype the Messiah, who builds the house of God's true worship. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch agree that the promise related primarily to Solomon, while its language of an everlasting throne reaches past him.

The wesleyan commentators fasten precisely on where the two levels part. Clarke observes that Solomon, not David, was to build the temple because David had shed blood abundantly and made great wars (1 Chronicles 22:8), and takes 'the throne of his kingdom for ever' as a reference to the spiritual kingdom of the Messiah, illustrated by the later prophet — 'of the increase of his government and peace there shall be no end, upon the throne of David' (Isaiah 9:7). Wesley draws the line most sharply: the house is meant literally of Solomon, who alone built the material temple, but ultimately of Christ, the builder of God's spiritual temple; and 'for ever' cannot be meant of Solomon, whose kingdom was not everlasting, but of David's posterity in general and with special respect to Christ, in whose person the kingdom was to be lodged for ever.

The debate among them is not whether the verse looks beyond Solomon — all grant that it does — but where the seam falls. Clarke and Wesley locate it exactly at 'for ever,' which they say bursts the bounds of any merely human reign; Gill and Keil hold the whole in a single typological frame, Solomon as the near term and the Messiah as the far. Wesley presses the point that the material temple, which Solomon 'alone did build,' answers to a spiritual house that Christ alone can raise, so that even the act of building carries the double sense the throne makes explicit. Either way the witnesses land together: the temple-builder of the near horizon is Solomon, but the everlasting throne he foreshadows belongs to David's greater Son, and the promise is designedly larger than the son who first fulfils it.
14:
'I will be his father, and he shall be my son' is the covenant-clause of the chapter, and the witnesses read it along a rising scale from Solomon to Christ, dividing over how far the promise of chastening can be carried. The reformed Gill takes the fatherhood first of Solomon, made God's son by adopting grace despite his failings, and then of Christ, the antitypical Solomon, who is Son of God in the higher sense of natural and eternal generation. The grammatical-historical Ellicott holds both together: the promise plainly has in view a human successor who may sin, yet by its emphatic threefold 'for ever' it also pledges an establishment that can only be fulfilled — and has always been understood to be fulfilled — in the Messiah. He compares the promise of a prophet 'human and yet more than human' in Deuteronomy 18:15-22, a succession of heads of the theocracy culminating in Him whose kingdom shall have no end (Luke 1:32-33).

On the clause 'if he commit iniquity, I will chasten him with the rod of men,' the wesleyan Wesley draws a firm boundary: this agrees only to Solomon and some others of David's posterity, and not at all to Christ, who never committed iniquity; here Solomon was no type of Christ, so this branch terminates in Solomon, whereas in the things where Solomon does typify Christ the sense passes through him to Christ. The 'rod of men,' Wesley and the puritan Matthew Henry agree, means gentle and moderate correction suited to man's weakness — the touches of the children of men, not the great power of God — an article of fatherly love, for what son is he whom the father chasteneth not.

Against this cautious reading stands the striking rendering Clarke defends in his long note on v.25: taking the Hebrew as a passive (niphal) form, he would translate not 'if he commit iniquity' but 'even in his suffering for iniquity,' so that the verse speaks of the Messiah chastised with the rod due to men for sins not his own — 'wounded for our transgressions... and with his stripes we are healed' (Isaiah 53:5). The witnesses thus offer a real division: most read the chastening of a sinning son and terminate it in Solomon and his line; Clarke boldly refers even this clause to the sinless Son bearing others' guilt. All agree on the covenant's warmth — fatherhood, sonship, correction in love, and mercy that will not be withdrawn.
15:
'But my mercy shall not depart from him, as I took it from Saul' turns the covenant's promise into an assurance of endurance, and here the witnesses openly divide over what kind of mercy is meant. The reformed Gill is careful to distinguish: this is not the special saving grace that can never be lost — though that is true of Solomon and every adopted child — but a mercy that can be taken from another, as it was from Saul; that is, the kingdom and its continuance in David's line. The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads it as a promise of permanence through discipline: Saul and his house were utterly and permanently set aside, but David's descendants, though punished for their sins, shall never be forgotten, and shall at last issue in one who conquers sin and death for ever. Keil and Delitzsch agree that the words relate primarily to Solomon while looking beyond.

The sharpest note is struck by the wesleyan Clarke, who uses the verse to close a door. David's house was a lasting house — Saul's family became totally extinct, David's remained until the incarnation, so that Joseph and Mary were both of it and Jesus the only heir to the throne of Israel, who chose the spiritual throne and now reigns exalted at God's right hand. But, Clarke warns, many have pressed these verses to support the doctrine of unconditional final perseverance, and 'with it the text has nothing to do'; indeed, were the point urged through the antitype Solomon, it would be ruined, for there is neither proof nor evidence of Solomon's salvation. Wesley, more briefly, glosses 'my mercy' as the kingdom mercifully promised, taken from Saul only as to his posterity, since the crown was continued to Saul's own person during his life.

The debate is genuine and denominational: Gill (with the Reformed instinct) hears in the abiding mercy a figure of covenant grace, while Clarke expressly forbids reading personal perseverance out of a promise about a throne. Yet even the disputants converge on the contrast that anchors the verse. What befell Saul will not befall David — the mercy that lit on Saul and was withdrawn will rest on David's house and remain, chastening its sins without cancelling its covenant, until it comes to rest for ever on the Son whose reign no sin can forfeit.
16:
'Thine house and thy kingdom shall be established for ever before thee' seals the oracle with the word the witnesses keep returning to — for ever — and they read it as reaching from Solomon's throne to the Messiah's. The reformed Gill hears both perpetuated together, family and government, so that David should always have one of his line upon the throne; he saw the beginning of it in Solomon and, by faith, its completion in his greater Son the Messiah, in whom alone the words find their full sense — noting that even the Jewish commentator Abarbinel refers the prophecy to the days of the Messiah.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott does the fine work. Two different Hebrew words are rendered 'established' in this verse; the second is elsewhere translated 'sure' (as in 1 Samuel 2:35; Isaiah 55:3) and would be better given here as 'made sure.' He defends the reading 'before thee' against the Septuagint's 'before me,' and then draws the verse into the sweep of redemptive history: David is now made the head of the line in which the primeval promise shall be fulfilled — the seed of the woman that shall bruise the serpent's head (Genesis 3:15), given first to the whole race, then narrowed to Abraham's seed (Genesis 22:18), then to the tribe of Judah (Genesis 49:10; compare Ezekiel 21:27), and now promised in the family of David.

The wesleyan Wesley lingers on 'before thee' — thine eyes in some sort beholding it — for David lived to see Solomon actually enthroned with general applause, an earnest of the kingdom's continuance, and beyond that he had his eyes and thoughts upon the Messiah (Ps. 110:1), whose day he saw by faith as Abraham did (John 8:56), knowing that God would raise up the fruit of his loins to sit on his throne for ever. The witnesses do not divide; together they make this the summit of the covenant — an everlasting house whose surety runs from Eden's first promise through Judah and David to Christ, so that the whole line of grace is gathered at last into one family and one endless throne.
17:
This quiet notice — 'according to all these words, and according to all this vision, so did Nathan speak unto David' — carries more weight than its brevity suggests, and the witnesses draw out two things from it: the fidelity of the prophet and the nature of the revelation. The reformed Gill fastens on the exactness: all the words of the prophecy, just as they were delivered to Nathan, were expressed by him; he did not vary from them in the least but related the whole with the greatest faithfulness. The point gains its force from the context. Only the day before, this same Nathan had counselled David out of his own judgment to go and build (v.3); now, with no defensiveness and no attempt to soften the reversal, he delivers word for word a message that overturns his own advice. The honest self-correction of a true prophet is written into the very completeness of his report.

The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the word 'vision.' Ellicott notes that the term is applied to any divine communication and not merely to a vision strictly so called (compare its use in Isaiah 1:1). Keil and Delitzsch sharpen this from the Hebrew: the revelation came to Nathan in a chizzayon, a waking vision by night, which Scripture consistently distinguishes from a chalom or dream — so that Nathan received the oracle not asleep and dreaming but awake and aware, in full possession of his faculties, which is precisely why he could report it with such precision.

The witnesses do not divide; the verse admits no controversy, and its lesson is the more powerful for its plainness. A prophet who had spoken hastily and been corrected the same night now proves his integrity by transmitting the correction entire, adding nothing and withholding nothing. The whole vision passes intact from God to Nathan to David, and the reader is assured that the great covenant just unfolded rests not on a dream or a private impression but on a waking word of God, faithfully carried by a man willing to unsay his own counsel for the sake of the truth.
18:
David's response — he 'went in, and sat before the Lord' — draws the witnesses first to the place and posture of his prayer, where a small exegetical debate arises. All agree on the setting: the reformed Gill and the wesleyan Wesley say he went into the tabernacle where the ark was, and the grammatical-historical Ellicott adds that, as at every important point of his life, David's first care was to take the matter before the Lord, here in the tent he had pitched for the ark. But the word 'sat' gives Keil and Delitzsch pause: the verb means 'remained' or 'tarried,' he argues, not literally 'sat,' for the custom of sitting as a posture of prayer cannot safely be built on this text. The puritan Matthew Henry records the Jewish tradition that it was permitted to the kings of David's house alone to sit in the sanctuary, but doubts it will justify the ordinary use of that gesture, and offers instead that David 'continued' before the Lord, composing himself in God's presence before he began — for so we should do in all our approaches to God.

The heart of the verse, on which the witnesses are wholly united, is its humility: 'Who am I, O Lord God, and what is my house, that thou hast brought me hitherto?' Henry marvels that a man so considerable — extraordinary in body and mind, a man of honour, success, and usefulness, the darling of his country and the dread of its enemies — should, when he comes before God, ask 'Who am I? a man not worth taking notice of,' and reckon his royal family poor, like Gideon thinking himself the least in his father's house (Judges 6:15). Gill and Wesley echo the note of unworthiness — how infinitely unworthy am I and my house of this honour.

Henry presses the lesson home: it well becomes the greatest and best of men, even amid the highest advancement, to have low thoughts of themselves, for the greatest are worms, the best are sinners, and the highest have nothing but what they received. That David traces his whole ascent to God ('thou hast brought me hitherto') the witnesses take as the root of true prayer: he could not have reached this by his own management, and all our attainments must be owned as God's vouchsafements. So the king who has just been promised an everlasting throne begins his thanksgiving by confessing he is nothing.
19:
This verse contains the chapter's hardest phrase, and the witnesses divide over it more than anywhere else: 'and is this the manner of man, O Lord God?' The dispute is over a single Hebrew word normally meaning 'law' (torah), which the Authorized Version renders 'manner.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott protests that the word never bears the sense 'manner' elsewhere; its well-established meaning is 'law,' and no question is really intended. He would translate it as an exclamation — 'And this is a law for man!' — David marvelling that so great a promise, a decree of an eternal kingdom, should be given to such as himself and his posterity; the parallel says it more flatly, 'thou hast regarded me according to the estate of a man of high degree' (1 Chronicles 17:17). Keil and Delitzsch take 'the law of man' differently, as the law God observes toward man — his love and condescension conformed to the rule men are to keep toward one another, 'thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself' (Leviticus 19:18; compare Micah 6:8).

The wesleyan Clarke turns the phrase toward prophecy. Literally, he says, it reads 'this is the law of Adam' — a reference to the promise made to Adam that the seed of the woman should bruise the serpent's head — so that David perceives the Messiah is to spring from his line, the everlasting King. Wesley, keeping closer to the surface, hears a wondering rhetorical question: do men deal so kindly with their inferiors as God has dealt with me? No — this is the prerogative of divine grace; and he glosses 'a great while to come' as reaching to all future ages, indeed to all eternity.

So the witnesses offer four keys to one lock: Ellicott's exclamation of wonder ('a law for man!'), Keil's law of neighbour-love that God himself keeps toward David, Clarke's law of Adam pointing to the promised Seed, and Wesley's astonished 'is this how men treat men?' They agree, however, on the emotion beneath the grammar. Whatever the exact rendering, David is overwhelmed that the eternal God should stoop to bind himself by promise to a shepherd and his house 'for a great while to come,' and every reading makes the verse a cry of amazement at a condescension without parallel among men.
20:
'And what can David say more unto thee? for thou, Lord God, knowest thy servant.' The witnesses read this as the point where thanksgiving passes into a reverent silence, and they weigh both halves of it. The reformed Gill hears David at a loss for words on three counts at once — in self-abasement, in thankfulness, and in prayer; his sense of his own nothingness and unworthiness outruns speech, and the grants already made are so large that there is no room left to ask for more. The wesleyan voices divide the sense the same way and settle it by the second clause. Wesley paraphrases: whether in gratitude ('words cannot express my obligations, nor my sense of them') or in petition ('what can I ask that thou hast not freely done?'), David falls back on 'thou knowest thy servant' — thou knowest my deep sense of thy favours, and thou knowest my condition and future need, and, knowing it, wilt surely supply it. Clarke compresses the whole to a sigh: 'How can I express my endless obligation to thee?'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch ground the silence theologically. Rather than multiply words, David appeals to the omniscience of God, before whom a thankful heart lies open, just as the psalmist does — 'O Lord, thou knowest' (Ps. 40:9; compare Ps. 17:3). Where language fails, the God who reads the heart has already heard what the tongue cannot frame.

The witnesses do not divide; they converge on a single movement of soul. The man who has just been promised an everlasting house finds that the promise has outrun his power of response, and instead of straining after adequate speech he rests in being fully known. This, the witnesses suggest, is the mark of true devotion — not that it says the most, but that it is content to be understood by One who needs no words, so that David's very speechlessness becomes the highest form of his praise and the surest ground of his hope that God, who knows his servant, will do for him more than he can ask.
21:
'For thy word's sake, and according to thine own heart, hast thou done all these great things.' The witnesses take this as David's own explanation of the whole covenant — it flows not from his desert but from God's word and God's heart — and they debate only which 'word' is meant. The reformed Gill offers a double possibility: either the former promise God made him by Samuel that he should be king, or, more deeply, the Messiah who should spring from him — the essential Word of God, which the Targum here calls the Memra, and which the Septuagint's reading 'for thy servant's sake' would support. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch require the 'word' to be some earlier promise of God, and canvass the options: Hengstenberg's reference to the charge 'arise, anoint him' (1 Samuel 16:12), which the parallel 'for thy servant's sake' seems to favour, or the older Messianic prophecies generally — the sceptre promised to Judah (Genesis 49:10) and the star and ruler out of Jacob (Numbers 24:17).

Both wings of the verse — 'thy word' and 'thine own heart' — are then read together as a denial of merit. The wesleyan Wesley glosses 'for thy word's sake' as 'that thou mightest fulfil thy promise and thereby demonstrate thy faithfulness,' and 'according to thine own heart' as 'of thy own mere liberality and good pleasure, without any desert of mine' — so far, he observes, was David, gracious man though he was, from thinking his actions meritorious. The puritan Matthew Henry sounds the same note: all is for the word's sake, for the sake of Christ the eternal Word, or that God might magnify his word of promise above all his name; and all is according to his own heart, his gracious counsel and good pleasure — 'even so, Father, because it seemed good in thy eyes.'

The debate over the referent of 'thy word' is friendly and unresolved — Samuel's anointing, the Messianic prophecies, or Christ the Word himself — and the witnesses feel no need to close it. What they insist on with one voice is the theology the verse enshrines: everything God has done and promised proceeds from his own utterance and his own heart, and nothing from David's worth. Grace is its own cause, and the covenant of an everlasting throne rests on the free good pleasure of God alone.
22:
'Wherefore thou art great, O Lord God, for there is none like thee.' David's thanksgiving now rises into pure adoration, and the witnesses follow him from the gifts to the Giver. The reformed Gill spreads out the greatness confessed: God is great in his name, nature, persons, and perfections, in his purposes, promises, and works. The wesleyan Wesley keeps it concrete — great both in power and in goodness, as appears by the great and good things God has done for him — so that David's doxology is drawn straight out of his own experience of mercy.

The puritan Matthew Henry marks the movement of soul this represents: God's gracious condescension to David, and the honour just put upon him, did not in the least abate his awe of the divine majesty; rather the nearer any are brought to God, the more they see of his glory, and the dearer we are in his eyes the greater he ought to be in ours. To be exalted by God, Henry observes, is no ground for familiarity but for deeper reverence.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott attends to the last clause, 'according to all that we have heard with our ears.' Such expressions, he notes, cover not merely what has been told orally but all that has been made known in any way, including the written record — the same word is used of things written in the sacred books (compare Deuteronomy 4:6; 2 Kings 19:16) — so that David is not appealing to hearsay tradition but to the whole recorded history of God's dealings with his people. Keil and Delitzsch, expounding the same clause, take the confession 'none like thee, neither any God beside thee' as the very heart of David's praise — the promise just given is proof of God's true and sole deity, worked out to glorify his name upon his chosen people. The witnesses do not divide; together they make this verse the hinge on which David turns from receiving to worshipping. The incomparable greatness of God is not an abstraction but the sum of what Israel has heard and read of his acts, and the proper response to an everlasting promise is to lose oneself in praise of the God who alone could make it and alone can keep it.
23:
'And what one nation in the earth is like thy people, even like Israel, whom God went to redeem?' David's wonder at God passes into wonder at God's people, and the witnesses read the verse for the uniqueness of Israel's redemption. The reformed Gill names what set Israel apart: the knowledge and worship of the true God among them, the laws given them, and the blessings of goodness bestowed on them — no other nation could show the like.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott does the textual and grammatical work, and finds it unusually knotty. The word for God stands here in its ordinary plural form but takes a plural verb — a construction he explains by the thought behind it: 'What nation is there whom its gods went to redeem?' The clause 'to do for you great things' seems strange addressed to God in a prayer, and Ellicott notes it was omitted by the Septuagint and changed by the Vulgate to 'for them,' though it may stand if David for a moment turns in thought to the people. He records the variant 'by driving out' for 'for thy land,' and observes that the whole passage is built upon the language of Deuteronomy — 'great things and terrible' echoing Deuteronomy 10:21, the whole founded on Deuteronomy 4:7 and Deuteronomy 4:32-34.

The richest reading comes from the puritan Matthew Henry, who hears the redemption of Israel as a type of the redemption by Christ. God 'went to redeem' as one setting about a great work; the plural 'Elohim went' Henry takes to hint at the concurrence of the persons of the Godhead in the work of redemption, as at creation when God said 'Let us make man.' And the redemption itself he reads figuratively: as Israel was redeemed from the nations and their gods to be a peculiar people, so believers are redeemed from all iniquity and conformity to this world; the honour of God and the eternal happiness of his people are the two ends of it. The witnesses do not really contend — Ellicott labouring at the grammar, Gill at Israel's privileges, Henry at the gospel foreshadowed — but they meet in one confession: no nation was ever so redeemed, and Israel's deliverance stands as the great earthly shadow of the redemption God works in Christ.
24:
'For thou hast confirmed to thyself thy people Israel to be a people unto thee for ever.' The witnesses read this as the covenant-clause of David's thanksgiving, and they weigh the firmness of the bond it names. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the establishing broadly: it refers not merely to the exodus or the conquest but to all that God had done to make Israel the people of his possession, from the days of Moses down to this very promise of an eternal throne; by all of it Jehovah had proved himself their God.

The wesleyan Wesley reads the confirmation as twofold in its means — partly by God's promises and the sure covenant he made with them, and partly by the glorious works wrought on their behalf, as it appears this day — and takes 'their God' to mean God by a peculiar and special relation and covenant, for otherwise he is the God and Father of all things. The puritan Matthew Henry catches both the reciprocity and the fixity of the tie: the covenant is mutual — 'they to be a people to thee, and thou to be a God to them,' all their interests consecrated to God and all his attributes engaged for them — and it is immutable, for 'thou hast confirmed them'; he that makes the covenant makes it sure and will make it good.

Only the reformed Gill introduces a note of qualification, and it is characteristic. The bond holds, he says, so long as Israel is obedient and abides by God's worship and ordinances; otherwise God would write a 'Lo-ammi' upon them — 'not my people' — as in the end he did. Here a gentle tension surfaces: where Wesley, Keil, and Henry stress the sureness and immutability of the covenant, Gill guards its conditional edge, the possibility of forfeiture that Israel's later history bore out. Yet the witnesses do not truly divide, for even Gill grounds the relation in God's own act of confirming, and all agree that the God who bound Israel to himself for ever is the same who now binds himself to David's house — the perpetuity of the people and the perpetuity of the throne resting alike on the faithfulness of the God who confirms.
25:
With 'And now, O Lord God, the word that thou hast spoken... establish it for ever, and do as thou hast said,' David turns from praise to petition, and the witnesses mark the movement. The reformed Gill notes the transition plainly — from confessions of unworthiness and recitals of God's goodness, David proceeds to his requests. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch show how the prayer is knit to the promise: because God had revealed that he would build David a house, therefore 'thy servant hath found in his heart' — that is, found joy and boldness — to pray this prayer; the request is nothing but the promise turned back to God as a plea.

The wesleyan Clarke fastens on the words 'do as thou hast said' and draws from them the note of conditionality. David well knew, he says, that all the promises made to himself and his family were conditional, and therefore he prays that they may be fulfilled; his posterity did not walk with God, and so were driven from the throne, the crown passing at last to other hands — so that all the promises have failed to David's natural line and are fulfilled only in Christ and his spiritual seed. Had David's posterity been faithful, Clarke reasons, they would be reigning still.

Around this petition Clarke hangs his longest reflection, and it supplies the verse's most memorable digression. Observing how seldom God employs a soldier in spiritual work — for the same reason given to David, that he was a man of blood — Clarke tells at length the story of John Haime, the Methodist preacher who served as a dragoon in Flanders, was in the hottest fire seven hours at Fontenoy, and yet was persuaded he never killed a man, his carbine misfiring and his horse turning aside, though he struck with all his might. God, Clarke concludes, had appointed him to build a spiritual house in the army and would not permit him to shed blood — a living parable of the principle that barred David from the temple. The witnesses do not contend over the petition itself; they agree that David rightly turns the promise into prayer. But Clarke stands somewhat apart in pressing the conditional character of the pledge and in reading David's disqualification, through the strange providence of a praying soldier, as a standing rule of how God assigns his holy work.
26:
'And let thy name be magnified for ever' lifts David's petition above his own house to the glory of God, and the witnesses hear in it the true center of all right prayer. The reformed Gill observes that David sought the performance of the promise not so much for his own sake or his family's as for the glory of God — his great concern was that God might be magnified and his greatness displayed in making David and his house great, and particularly that God might be glorified in that famous Son of his, the Messiah, and by all his people in succeeding ages.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott makes the same point the key to David's whole life: here, in the true spirit of the Lord's Prayer, David puts in the forefront of his petition the 'hallowed be thy name,' and this is the striking feature of all his life — into whatever sins he was at times betrayed, his main object was to live to the glory of God. The wesleyan Wesley reads 'let thy name be magnified' as a prayer that God would never cease to manifest himself to be the God and governor of Israel.

The puritan Matthew Henry gives the thought its fullest frame. This ought to be the summary and center of all our prayers, their Alpha and Omega: begin with 'Hallowed be thy name' and end with 'Thine is the glory for ever'; 'whether I be magnified or no, let thy name be magnified.' And Henry notes what magnifies it — to say, with suitable affection, that 'the Lord of hosts is the God over Israel,' which bespeaks him gloriously great and gloriously good at once. He hears the Son of David praying in the same key: 'Father, glorify thy name' (John 12:28), and 'glorify thy Son, that thy Son also may glorify thee' (John 17:1). The witnesses do not divide; they agree that David desired the promise fulfilled not for his own honour but for God's, so that the highest reach of the whole prayer is not the security of a dynasty but the magnifying of the name of God for ever.
27:
'For thou, O Lord of hosts, God of Israel, hast revealed to thy servant, saying, I will build thee an house: therefore hath thy servant found in his heart to pray this prayer unto thee.' The witnesses read this verse as David's account of what makes his bold prayer possible: it is authorized by God's own revelation. The grammatical-historical Ellicott states the principle it teaches — the ground of the believer's prayer must ever be the lovingkindness and the promises of God; David asks great things only because God has first spoken them. Keil and Delitzsch, expounding the same clause, render 'found in his heart to pray' as 'found joy' in praying: the revelation did not merely permit the prayer but gladdened and emboldened it.

The puritan Matthew Henry unfolds this at length and makes it the model of all believing prayer. David grounds his petitions on the message God had sent him: 'thou hast of thine own good will given me the promise that thou wilt build me a house, else I could never have found in my heart to pray such a prayer as this.' He would not have dared to ask such great things had he not been directed and encouraged by the promise to ask them — 'they are indeed too great for me to beg, but not too great for thee to give.' Henry lingers on the phrase 'found in his heart,' which in the original, he notes, means the heart was found — fixed, gathered in from its wanderings, and entirely engaged in the duty. Many, when they go to pray, have their hearts to seek; but David's heart was found. That prayer which is found in the tongue only will not please God; it must be found in the heart, which must be lifted up and poured out before him.

The reformed Gill simply anchors the address in the title 'Lord of hosts, God of Israel.' The witnesses do not divide; they converge on a single truth about prayer. We pray rightly and boldly only what God has revealed himself willing to give, so that his promise is both the warrant and the very substance of our asking — and the heart that has heard God's word, and is fixed upon it, is the heart from which acceptable prayer arises.
28:
'And now, O Lord God, thou art that God, and thy words be true, and thou hast promised this goodness unto thy servant.' The witnesses read this as the plank on which David's whole hope rests: the trustworthiness of God and the truth of his word. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note how the verse gathers up the two halves of the prayer — with the twin clauses opening 'and now,' David sums up his thanksgiving by celebrating the greatness of the Lord and his promise, and then his petition. The reformed Gill expands 'thou art that God' by his titles: he who is the Lord of hosts and the God of Israel, who has promised, and is able to perform, and is faithful to his promise — the three grounds of confidence, power and truth and covenant faithfulness, standing together.

The wesleyan Wesley reads 'that God' relationally: that God who has declared himself to be Israel's God, and in particular David's own God. The force of the demonstrative is not abstract deity but the covenant nearness of a God who has bound himself to this people and this man; the same emphatic 'thou art that God' that opens the verse is, on Wesley's reading, less a definition of the divine essence than a grateful naming of the God whose word David has just heard and now trusts.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the practical use for faith. David here builds his faith, and his hope to speed in prayer, upon the fidelity of God's promise: 'thou art that God whose words are true, that God whom one may depend upon,' and 'thou hast promised this goodness, which I am therefore bold to pray for.' The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the pivot of the verse is the word 'true.' Having asked God to do as he has said (v.25), David now rests the asking on the certainty that God's sayings are not empty — for with God, as Henry elsewhere observes, saying and doing are not two things as they often are with men. The everlasting covenant is safe because the God who spoke it cannot lie, and David's confidence is not in his own worthiness but in the veracity of the promiser.
29:
The prayer closes as it must — 'now therefore let it please thee to bless the house of thy servant, that it may continue for ever before thee' — and the witnesses gather its threads into a final benediction resting wholly on grace. The reformed Gill insists on the ground: David asks the blessing not according to the merits of himself or his family but according to the sovereign will and pleasure of God, and he cites the Targum's rendering, 'begin and bless,' as a plea that the promised blessings might begin to descend as an earnest and pledge that the whole will be fulfilled. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, note how the verse rounds off the two movements of the prayer, the closing 'with thy blessing let the house of thy servant be blessed for ever' answering to the promise with which the whole began.

Ellicott raises a small but suggestive question of mood. 'Let it please thee' may be taken as an optative, as in the Authorized Version, or better as a future — 'It will please thee' — so that the closing words become not merely a wish but a confident prophecy founded on the promise, much as the last clause of the Te Deum may be read either 'let me never be confounded' or 'I shall never be confounded.' He adds that several Psalms take their keynote from this ever-fresh promise, above all the one that reads like an inspired interpretation of the perpetuity of David's kingdom (Psalms 110:1).

The wesleyan Wesley and the puritan Matthew Henry carry the 'for ever' to its term in Christ. Wesley says the prayer was abundantly answered when Christ for ever sat down at the right hand of God and received all assurance that his seed and throne should be as the days of heaven. Henry, expounding the same close, prays that the entail of the crown might remain in David's family and find its perfection and perpetuity in the kingdom of the Messiah, fulfilled when Christ sat down at God's right hand (Hebrews 10:12) and 'this prayer of David the son of Jesse for his seed was abundantly answered' (compare Psalms 72:17). The witnesses do not divide; they end where the chapter aims — a house blessed not by desert but by God's good pleasure, its blessing an earnest now and its perpetuity secured for ever in the enthroned Son of David, so that David's last petition and God's first promise meet and are answered in Christ.
